triage.clinical_question: Is the smartwatch tachycardia reading likely artifact or a true elevated heart rate requiring clinical review, given the high-motion/poor-contact context and recent lower trend readings?
decision_factors: Heart rate of 148 bpm exceeds the supplied high threshold of 130 bpm.
Recent trend shows substantially lower values over the prior 10 minutes (78 to 94 bpm), suggesting possible transient spike.
High motion and poor skin contact make device artifact likely, but the alert still warrants review.
Alert category is device_artifact, which lowers certainty but does not fully dismiss the outlier reading.

retrieval_query.clinical_question: Is the smartwatch tachycardia reading likely artifact or a true elevated heart rate requiring clinical review, given the high-motion/poor-contact context and recent lower trend readings?

relevant_notes.title: Palpitations review
relevant_notes.text: Prior ECG showed sinus rhythm. Patient advised to document activity and device fit during alerts.

missing_data: No actual smartwatch heart rate value, duration, symptoms, or contemporaneous ECG/clinical assessment provided.
No explicit documentation of motion artifact or device contact quality beyond the user's context.

symptoms_and_timeline: 2026-05-12: Felt briefly winded after climbing stairs.
Denies chest pain, fainting, or persistent palpitations.
lifestyle_factors: 2026-05-12: The smartwatch alert occurred during vigorous activity.
2026-05-12: Watch strap was loose and showed poor skin contact.
patient_concerns: Asks whether the watch reading could be inaccurate.

brief.alert_summary: Smartwatch recorded a heart rate of 148 bpm, above the high threshold, but the alert occurred during vigorous activity with high motion and poor skin contact, and the recent trend was lower. The reading is more consistent with possible device artifact than a confirmed sustained tachycardia, but it still warrants clinical review.
brief.patient_snapshot: 36-year-old female with active generalized anxiety disorder and ongoing sertraline use. Prior palpitations review documented sinus rhythm on ECG and advised documenting activity and device fit during alerts. Recent anamnesis notes brief windedness after stairs, no chest pain, no fainting, and no persistent palpitations. The alert occurred during vigorous activity with a loose strap and poor skin contact.

contextual_analysis.text: The alert value of 148 bpm exceeds the device threshold of 130 bpm, so it cannot be dismissed outright.
The device context strongly supports artifact risk: the alert was labeled as device_artifact, with high motion and poor skin contact at the time of the reading.
The recent trend shows substantially lower heart rates in the prior 10 minutes, which makes a transient spike or measurement error more plausible than a sustained elevation.
Prior evaluation for palpitations found sinus rhythm on ECG, which lowers concern for a previously documented arrhythmia history in the available record.
Current anamnesis does not report chest pain, syncope, or persistent palpitations; this reduces immediate concern for an unstable clinical event based on the available history.
Anxiety is an active condition and can complicate symptom interpretation, but the available data do not support a diagnosis from this alert alone.

risk_assessment.text: Main risk is over-calling an artifact as a true tachycardia, which could lead to unnecessary escalation.
A true elevated heart rate cannot be excluded from a single wearable reading, especially if the patient was exercising at the time.
Medication review is relevant because sertraline is active therapy, but the available record does not identify it as the cause of the alert.
Absence of chest pain, fainting, or persistent palpitations lowers immediate risk in the available history, though monitoring remains appropriate.

recommended_actions.action: Have the patient repeat the heart rate measurement after resting, with the watch repositioned for better skin contact, and document whether symptoms are present.
recommended_actions.rationale: This helps distinguish a transient wearable artifact from a persistent elevated rate using the same device context noted in the record.

recommended_actions.action: Review for any associated symptoms at the time of the alert, especially chest pain, fainting, persistent palpitations, or worsening shortness of breath.
recommended_actions.rationale: Symptoms would raise concern for a clinically relevant event and change the urgency of follow-up.

recommended_actions.action: If repeat readings remain elevated at rest or symptoms develop, route for clinician review with consideration of contemporaneous clinical assessment.
recommended_actions.rationale: A persistent resting elevation would be less consistent with artifact and deserves human review.

recommended_actions.action: Continue to ask the patient to document activity level and watch fit during future alerts.
recommended_actions.rationale: This was previously advised and is directly useful for interpreting recurrent wearable alerts.

uncertainties_and_gaps: No contemporaneous ECG or clinician-measured vital signs were provided.
No exact duration of the 148 bpm reading is available.
The record does not include a confirmed resting repeat measurement after the alert.
There is no direct clinical assessment at the time of the smartwatch event.
brief.disclaimer: Educational prototype using simulated data. This output is not a diagnosis or medical advice and must be reviewed by a qualified clinician.
